1:
"Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where was a synagogue of the Jews." The witnesses note Paul's arrival at Thessalonica. The grammatical voices note the route: from Philippi through Amphipolis and Apollonia (stations on the Egnatian Way) to Thessalonica, the chief city of Macedonia, where there was a synagogue of the Jews.

On the synagogue, the witnesses note Paul's regular method: beginning where there was a synagogue, that he might reason from the Scriptures with the Jews and God-fearers. Thessalonica, a major commercial city, had a Jewish community.

The puritan Henry and others note the providence in the route: Paul pressing on to the strategic cities, where the gospel might spread widely. The verse opens the Thessalonian ministry.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, traveling the Egnatian Way through Amphipolis and Apollonia, came to Thessalonica, the chief Macedonian city, where there was a synagogue — his regular starting-point. The verse displays Paul's method: beginning at the synagogue, where he could reason from the Scriptures. The verse marks the opening of the Thessalonian ministry: Paul arriving at a major city with a Jewish community. The verse displays the strategic route: the apostle pressing on to the important cities where the gospel might spread. The verse displays the providence in Paul's movements: passing through lesser towns to reach Thessalonica, where a church would be founded. The synagogue, Paul's regular base, would be the scene of his reasoning (v.2) and the gathering of the first converts. The verse, brief as it is, opens the account of the Thessalonian church, to which Paul would write his earliest epistles. The verse displays the apostle's consistent strategy: beginning at the synagogue, reasoning from the Scriptures, gathering Jews and God-fearers. The arrival at Thessalonica marks a significant stage in the Macedonian mission, the founding of a church that would become a model of faith (1 Thessalonians 1:7). Paul's coming to the chief city, where there was a synagogue, sets the scene for the reasoning, the conversions, and the opposition that would follow.
2:
"And Paul, as his manner was, went in unto them, and three sabbath days reasoned with them out of the scriptures," The witnesses note Paul's customary method. On "as his manner was," the grammatical voices note his regular practice: entering the synagogue first. Robertson and others note Paul always began with the Jews, reasoning from the Scriptures they shared.

On "three sabbath days reasoned," the witnesses note the sustained argument. Calvin: Paul "reasoned" (dialegomai) — disputed, discoursed, argued from the Scriptures, over three sabbaths. The reasoning was from the Old Testament, the common ground with the Jews.

On "out of the scriptures," the witnesses note the foundation. Paul grounded his argument in the Scriptures, proving from them that the Messiah must suffer and rise (v.3). The verse displays the apostle's scriptural method.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, as his custom was, entered the synagogue and over three sabbaths reasoned from the Scriptures — his regular method of beginning with the Jews (Robertson), disputing and discoursing from the Old Testament (Calvin), the common ground. The verse displays Paul's scriptural method: reasoning from the Scriptures the Jews accepted. The verse marks the sustained argument: three sabbaths of reasoning, proving the gospel from the Scriptures. The verse displays the apostle's consistent practice: grounding his preaching in the Scriptures, arguing from them that Jesus is the Christ. The verse displays the foundation of Paul's gospel: not novel speculation but the fulfillment of the Scriptures. The reasoning "out of the scriptures" displays the unity of the Old and New Testaments: Paul proving from the prophets that the Messiah must suffer and rise. The verse displays the model of gospel preaching: reasoning from the Scriptures, persuading from the sacred text. The three sabbaths of reasoning display Paul's patient, sustained effort to convince the Jews from their own Scriptures. The verse sets up the content of his argument (v.3): that Christ must suffer and rise, and that Jesus is the Christ. Paul's scriptural method, grounding the gospel in the Old Testament, is the pattern of apostolic preaching to the Jews.
3:
"Opening and alleging, that Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again from the dead; and that this Jesus, whom I preach unto you, is Christ." The witnesses note the substance of Paul's argument. On "opening and alleging," the grammatical voices note the method. Robertson and Barnes note Paul "opened" (explained, expounded) the Scriptures and "alleged" (set forth, laid before them) the proofs — expounding the prophecies and applying them.

On "Christ must needs have suffered, and risen again," the witnesses note the necessity. The Messiah, according to the Scriptures, must suffer and rise — contrary to the Jewish expectation of a triumphant Messiah. Paul proved this necessity from the prophets (Isaiah 53, Psalm 16).

On "this Jesus is Christ," the witnesses note the application. Having proved that the Messiah must suffer and rise, Paul identified Jesus as that Messiah — the suffering and risen one fulfilling the prophecies. The verse displays the heart of Paul's gospel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul opened (expounded) and alleged (set forth) from the Scriptures that the Christ must suffer and rise, and that Jesus is that Christ — expounding the prophecies and applying them (Robertson, Barnes). The necessity of the Messiah's suffering and rising (contrary to Jewish expectation) was proved from the prophets (Isaiah 53, Psalm 16); and Jesus was identified as the suffering and risen Messiah. The verse displays the heart of Paul's gospel: the suffering and risen Christ, proved from the Scriptures, identified as Jesus. The verse marks the substance of the synagogue reasoning: the Messiah must suffer and rise, and Jesus is he. The verse displays the two-fold argument: first, that the Scriptures require a suffering and rising Messiah; second, that Jesus fulfills this. The verse displays the centrality of the cross and resurrection: the suffering and rising of the Christ, the heart of the gospel. The verse displays Paul's method of proof: opening the Scriptures, alleging the prophecies, applying them to Jesus. The argument — that Christ must suffer and rise, and that Jesus is the Christ — is the core of apostolic preaching, grounded in the Scriptures and centered on the suffering and risen Lord. The verse displays the gospel Paul preached: the crucified and risen Jesus, the fulfillment of the Messianic prophecies.
4:
"And some of them believed, and consorted with Paul and Silas; and of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and of the chief women not a few." The witnesses note the converts at Thessalonica. The grammatical voices note the three groups: some of the Jews, a great multitude of the devout Greeks (God-fearers), and not a few of the chief women.

On the devout Greeks, the witnesses note the God-fearers — Gentiles attending the synagogue, drawn to the God of Israel. These embraced the gospel in great numbers, more readily than the Jews.

On the chief women, the witnesses note the prominent women converted. As elsewhere (Acts 13:50, 17:12), women of rank embraced the faith. The verse displays the fruit of Paul's reasoning: a mixed church of Jews, God-fearers, and notable women.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the converts were three groups — some Jews, a great multitude of devout Greeks (God-fearers drawn to the God of Israel), and not a few chief women (women of rank, as elsewhere). The verse displays the fruit of Paul's reasoning: a mixed church gathered from Jews, God-fearers, and prominent women. The verse marks the founding of the Thessalonian church: the first converts joining Paul and Silas. The verse displays the breadth of the harvest: Jews believing, but the God-fearers in great numbers, and notable women. The verse displays the typical pattern: fewer Jews, more God-fearers, and women of rank — the composition of the early churches. The verse displays the beginning of the Thessalonian church, which would become a model of faith (1 Thessalonians 1:7). The "consorted with Paul and Silas" displays the converts joining the apostles, casting in their lot with them. The verse displays the gospel's appeal across groups: Jews, Greeks, and women, gathered into one church. The harvest at Thessalonica — Jews, God-fearers, and chief women — displays the fruit of Paul's scriptural reasoning and the founding of a flourishing church. The verse sets up the opposition (v.5): the success provoking the unbelieving Jews to envy and assault. The verse displays both the fruit and the foreshadowed conflict of the Thessalonian mission.
5:
"But the Jews which believed not, moved with envy, took unto them certain lewd fellows of the baser sort, and gathered a company, and set all the city on an uproar, and assaulted the house of Jason, and sought to bring them out to the people." The witnesses note the Jews' envious assault. On "moved with envy," the grammatical voices note the motive. The unbelieving Jews, jealous of Paul's success (especially among the God-fearers they had hoped to win), stirred up trouble.

On "lewd fellows of the baser sort," the witnesses note the rabble. Robertson and others note the Jews enlisted worthless men from the marketplace (agoraioi) — idlers and ruffians — to incite a mob. Unable to refute Paul, they resorted to violence.

On the assault on Jason's house, the witnesses note the target. Jason had hosted Paul; the mob, seeking the apostles, attacked his house. The Reformed Calvin notes the perennial pattern: the gospel's success provoking the world's rage. The verse displays the envious, violent opposition.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the unbelieving Jews, moved with envy at Paul's success (especially among the God-fearers), enlisted worthless rabble (agoraioi, idlers and ruffians, Robertson) to incite a mob and assault Jason's house (Paul's host), seeking the apostles. Calvin notes the perennial pattern: the gospel's success provoking the world's rage. The verse displays the envious, violent opposition: the Jews, unable to refute Paul, resorting to mob violence. The verse marks the outbreak of persecution at Thessalonica: envy issuing in assault. The verse displays the motive of the opposition: envy at the gospel's success, the loss of the God-fearers to Paul. The verse displays the means: enlisting the rabble, inciting a mob, attacking the host. The verse displays the typical course of persecution: the unbelieving Jews, defeated in argument, resorting to violence. The envious assault, the rabble, the attack on Jason's house display the malice that pursued the apostles. The verse displays the cost of the gospel's success: the persecution that followed the harvest. The envious, violent opposition at Thessalonica displays the world's enmity against the gospel and the suffering of the early church. The verse sets up the charges before the rulers (vv.6-7) and the security given by Jason (v.9).
6:
"And when they found them not, they drew Jason and certain brethren unto the rulers of the city, crying, These that have turned the world upside down are come hither also;" The witnesses note the famous charge against the apostles. On "turned the world upside down," the grammatical voices note the testimony to the gospel's impact. Barnes and others note the phrase, though hostile, witnesses to the power and spread of the gospel — the apostles had indeed shaken the world.

On the dragging of Jason, the witnesses note the substitution. Not finding Paul and Silas, the mob seized Jason and other brethren, dragging them before the politarchs (city rulers).

The witnesses note the irony in the charge. The gospel had "turned the world upside down" — not destroying but transforming, overturning idolatry and sin. The Reformed Calvin notes the accusation, meant as reproach, was in truth the highest praise: the gospel revolutionizing the world. The verse displays the charge that testified to the gospel's power.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: not finding Paul and Silas, the mob dragged Jason and other brethren before the city rulers (politarchs), charging "these that have turned the world upside down are come hither also" — a hostile accusation that nonetheless testifies to the gospel's power and spread (Barnes). Calvin notes the charge, meant as reproach, was in truth high praise: the gospel revolutionizing the world. The verse displays the famous charge: the apostles "turning the world upside down." The verse marks the testimony of the gospel's impact: even the enemies acknowledged its power to transform the world. The verse displays the irony: the accusation of disruption was in truth the witness of the gospel's transforming force, overturning idolatry and sin. The verse displays the spread of the gospel: from Jerusalem to Thessalonica, the apostles had shaken the world. The phrase "turned the world upside down," though hostile, is one of the great testimonies to the gospel's revolutionary power — not destroying but transforming, overturning the old order of sin and idolatry. The verse displays both the malice of the accusers and the unintended tribute to the gospel. The charge, dragging Jason before the rulers, displays the persecution; yet the words testify to the gospel's mighty advance. The verse displays the world-transforming power of the gospel, acknowledged even by its enemies.
7:
"Whom Jason hath received: and these all do contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, one Jesus." The witnesses note the political charge against the apostles. On "contrary to the decrees of Caesar," the grammatical voices note the accusation of treason. The Jews charged that Paul preached "another king, one Jesus" — a charge of sedition against Rome, calculated to alarm the rulers.

On "another king, one Jesus," the witnesses note the distortion. Paul preached Jesus as king (the Messiah, the King of the spiritual kingdom); the Jews twisted this into a political claim, as if Jesus rivaled Caesar. The same charge had been brought against Christ (Luke 23:2; John 19:12).

The witnesses note the malice and cunning of the charge. By framing the gospel as treason, the Jews sought to turn the Roman authorities against the apostles. The verse displays the political accusation, echoing the charge against Jesus.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Jews charged that the apostles acted contrary to Caesar's decrees, "saying that there is another king, one Jesus" — a charge of sedition, distorting Paul's preaching of Jesus as the Messianic king into a political rivalry with Caesar (as the same charge against Christ, Luke 23:2; John 19:12). The verse displays the political accusation: framing the gospel as treason. The verse marks the cunning of the charge: calculated to alarm the Roman rulers. The verse displays the distortion: Paul preached Jesus as king of a spiritual kingdom, but the Jews twisted it into a claim against Caesar. The verse displays the parallel with the charge against Jesus: "another king" rivaling Caesar, the accusation that had doomed the Lord. The verse displays the malice of the accusers: framing the gospel as sedition to provoke the authorities. The political charge, "another king, one Jesus," displays both the distortion of the gospel and the truth it twisted — for Jesus is indeed King, though of a kingdom not of this world. The verse displays the persecution's strategy: turning the gospel's truth (Jesus is King) into a charge of treason. The verse displays the suffering of the apostles, falsely accused of sedition, sharing the charge brought against their Lord. The political accusation, echoing the charge against Christ, displays the malice of the enemies and the providential parallel between the apostles and their King.
8:
"And they troubled the people and the rulers of the city, when they heard these things." The brief verse notes the alarm caused by the charges. The grammatical voices note the effect: the charge of sedition (another king than Caesar) troubled both the populace and the city rulers (politarchs), who feared Roman displeasure.

On the trouble, the witnesses note the gravity of the accusation. A charge of treason against Caesar was serious; the rulers, responsible to Rome, were alarmed and had to act. The verse displays the disturbance the charge created.

The Reformed Calvin notes the providence even in the trouble: the rulers, though troubled, did not condemn the apostles but required security (v.9), a milder outcome. The verse displays the alarm and the providential restraint.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the charge of sedition troubled both the people and the city rulers (politarchs), who feared Roman displeasure at the report of "another king." The gravity of the treason charge alarmed the rulers, responsible to Rome. Calvin notes the providence: though troubled, the rulers did not condemn the apostles but required only security (v.9), a milder outcome. The verse displays the disturbance the charge created: the populace and rulers alarmed. The verse marks the effect of the accusation: the serious charge of treason troubling the city. The verse displays the gravity of the political charge: a claim of "another king" threatening Roman order. The verse displays the providence in the trouble: the rulers alarmed yet restrained, requiring security rather than condemning. The verse displays the danger the apostles faced: a charge of sedition before authorities responsible to Rome. Yet the providence that watched over the mission restrained the outcome: the rulers troubled but not vengeful. The verse displays the disturbance and the providential limit: the charge alarming the city, yet not issuing in the apostles' destruction. The verse marks the tension of the moment: the serious accusation, the troubled rulers, the danger to Jason and the brethren. The verse displays the providence preserving the mission even amid the alarm, leading to the milder resolution of security given (v.9).
9:
"And when they had taken security of Jason, and of the other, they let them go." The witnesses note the resolution: security given, the brethren released. The grammatical voices note the legal arrangement. Robertson and others note "security" (a bond, surety) was taken from Jason and the others — a pledge, perhaps that Paul would leave or that there would be no further disturbance.

On the release, the witnesses note the milder outcome. The rulers, satisfied with the security, let the brethren go — not condemning them, but requiring a pledge. This was a relatively favorable result.

The witnesses note the providence and the consequence. The security may have required Paul's departure (v.10), explaining his leaving by night. The verse displays the resolution that preserved the brethren while sending Paul on.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the rulers took security (a bond, surety) from Jason and the others — a pledge, perhaps that there would be no further disturbance or that Paul would leave — and released the brethren (Robertson). The verse displays the milder outcome: security given, the brethren freed, not condemned. The verse marks the resolution of the crisis: the rulers satisfied with a pledge. The verse displays the providence in the outcome: a relatively favorable result, the brethren preserved. The verse displays the consequence: the security may have required Paul's departure, explaining his leaving by night (v.10). The verse displays the legal arrangement: a bond taken, the brethren released, the disturbance settled. The verse displays the providence preserving the church at Thessalonica: Jason and the brethren freed, the gospel rooted despite the persecution. The security, while sending Paul on, left the church established. The verse displays the favorable resolution: not condemnation but a pledge, the brethren spared. The verse marks the close of the Thessalonian crisis: the security given, the brethren released, Paul about to depart for Berea. The providential outcome — a pledge rather than punishment — displays God's care preserving the brethren and the church amid the persecution. The verse displays the resolution that protected the believers while moving the apostle on to the next field.
10:
"And the brethren immediately sent away Paul and Silas by night unto Berea: who coming thither went into the synagogue of the Jews." The witnesses note Paul's departure to Berea. The puritan Henry and the grammatical voices note the prudent night departure. The brethren, for Paul's safety (perhaps required by the security), sent him and Silas away by night to Berea.

On Berea, the witnesses note it was a city of Macedonia, somewhat off the main road. Paul, as ever, went to the synagogue — his regular method, beginning with the Jews.

The witnesses note the providence in the move: the persecution at Thessalonica driving Paul to Berea, where the noble Bereans awaited (v.11). The verse displays the prudent departure and the continued mission.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the brethren, for safety, sent Paul and Silas away by night to Berea, where Paul again went to the synagogue — the prudent night departure (Henry) and his regular method of beginning with the Jews. The verse displays the providential move: the persecution driving Paul to Berea. The verse marks the transition to the Berean ministry: Paul, sent away by night, continuing his work at the synagogue. The verse displays the prudence of the brethren: protecting Paul by a night departure. The verse displays the apostle's consistency: at Berea, as everywhere, beginning at the synagogue. The verse displays the providence in the persecution: driving Paul to Berea, where the noble Bereans would receive the word more nobly (v.11). The verse displays the resilience of the mission: opposition at Thessalonica only moving Paul to a new field. The night departure, the arrival at Berea, the synagogue ministry display the continued advance of the gospel despite persecution. The verse displays God's providence turning persecution into the gospel's spread: Paul driven from Thessalonica to Berea, where a more receptive people awaited. The verse marks the providential transition to the Berean ministry, where Paul's method would meet a noble response (v.11). The verse displays both the cost of the gospel (Paul fleeing by night) and the providence that carried it forward to new and receptive hearers.
11:
"These were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness of mind, and searched the scriptures daily, whether those things were so." The witnesses note the noble Bereans — the model of testing teaching by Scripture. On "more noble," the grammatical voices note the quality of mind. Barnes: "more generous, liberal, and noble in their feelings; more disposed to inquire candidly into the truth." Ellicott: "the generous, loyal temper... not the slaves of prejudice." The nobility was of disposition, not birth.

On "received the word with all readiness," the witnesses note their openness. They welcomed the gospel eagerly, with willingness to learn — yet not credulously.

On "searched the scriptures daily," the witnesses note the model of examination. Barnes draws the lessons: it is noble to examine the truth; the Scriptures should be searched daily; preaching should be tested by the Bible. The Reformed Calvin notes the balance: not judging arrogantly, but examining Paul's doctrine by the Scripture, the touchstone — refuting the Papist claim that faith rests on the church's authority, for the Bereans were commended for searching the Scriptures themselves. Robertson: "Paul's preaching made Bible students of them."

The witnesses agree: the Bereans were more noble than the Thessalonians — noble in disposition (generous, candid, unprejudiced, Barnes, Ellicott) — receiving the word eagerly yet searching the Scriptures daily to test whether Paul's teaching was true. Barnes draws the lessons: the nobility of examining the truth, the duty of daily Scripture study, the testing of preaching by the Bible. Calvin notes the balance: not arrogant judgment but examination by the Scripture, the touchstone — refuting the Papist claim that faith rests on church authority, for the Bereans searched themselves. Robertson: Paul's preaching made Bible students of them. The verse displays the model of the noble Berean: open yet discerning, eager yet examining. The verse marks the great example of testing teaching by Scripture: the Bereans receiving the word readily, yet proving it by the Scriptures. The verse displays the balance of faith and reason: neither credulous nor skeptical, but receiving with readiness and examining with care. The verse displays the duty of private examination: testing all teaching by the Scriptures. The noble Bereans, the model of discerning faith, display the right relation of reason and revelation: welcoming the word, yet proving it by the sacred text. The verse is the classic charter of Scripture-testing: every doctrine to be examined by the Bible, the standard of truth.
12:
"Therefore many of them believed; also of honourable women which were Greeks, and of men, not a few." The witnesses note the fruit of the Bereans' examination. On "therefore many believed," the grammatical voices note the result. Barnes: "As the result of their examination. They found that the doctrines of Paul accorded with the Old Testament." The careful searching led to faith.

On the lesson, Barnes notes: "This result will commonly follow when people search the Scriptures... we may commonly take it for granted that such an examination will result in their conviction of the truth." Honest examination of the Scriptures leads to faith.

On the converts, the witnesses note the breadth: many Jews, honorable Greek women, and men. The careful examination of the Bereans issued in a rich harvest. The verse displays the fruit of Scripture-searching faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: many Bereans believed as the result of their searching the Scriptures (finding Paul's doctrine accorded with the Old Testament, Barnes), along with honorable Greek women and men. Barnes draws the lesson: honest examination of the Scriptures commonly leads to faith — the searching of the Bereans issuing in conviction. The verse displays the fruit of Scripture-searching faith: many believing after examining the word. The verse marks the rich harvest at Berea: Jews, women, and men converted. The verse displays the connection between examination and faith: the Bereans, having searched the Scriptures, believed. The verse displays the lesson: honest inquiry into the Scriptures leads to conviction of the truth. The verse displays the breadth of the harvest: many Jews (more than at Thessalonica), honorable women, and men. The verse displays the fruit of the noble Berean disposition: the careful searching issuing in genuine, well-grounded faith. The verse displays the model of conversion: receiving the word, examining the Scriptures, and believing on the evidence. The harvest at Berea — many converts of all kinds — displays the reward of the noble, examining spirit: faith grounded in the Scriptures. The verse displays the providence in the Berean ministry: the receptive, discerning people yielding a rich harvest. The verse marks the fruit of Scripture-testing faith — the Bereans, having proved the word, believing in large numbers.
13:
"But when the Jews of Thessalonica had knowledge that the word of God was preached of Paul at Berea, they came thither also, and stirred up the people." The witnesses note the Thessalonian Jews pursuing Paul. The grammatical voices note the relentless malice. The unbelieving Jews of Thessalonica, hearing of Paul's success at Berea, came there to stir up trouble — pursuing him beyond their own city.

On the stirring up, the witnesses note the same tactic as at Thessalonica (v.5): inciting the populace against Paul. The Jews, unable to refute him, again resorted to mob agitation.

The Reformed Calvin notes the perennial enmity: the gospel's success provoking the enemies to follow and oppose it. The verse displays the persistent persecution pursuing Paul to Berea.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the Thessalonian Jews, hearing of Paul's success at Berea, came there and stirred up the people — pursuing him beyond their own city, using the same tactic of mob agitation as before (v.5). Calvin notes the perennial enmity: the gospel's success provoking the enemies to follow and oppose it. The verse displays the persistent persecution: the Jews pursuing Paul to Berea. The verse marks the relentless malice: the enemies following the apostle to disrupt his work. The verse displays the same pattern: unable to refute Paul, the Jews inciting the populace. The verse displays the cost of the gospel's success: drawing the persecutors after it. The verse displays the determination of Paul's enemies: traveling to Berea to oppose him. The persistent persecution, pursuing Paul from city to city, displays the world's enmity against the gospel. The verse displays the providence amid the opposition: the persecution would drive Paul on to Athens (v.15), spreading the gospel further. The verse displays the relentless malice of the Thessalonian Jews, following Paul to stir up trouble at Berea. The verse marks the renewed opposition that would send Paul to Athens, the great center of philosophy, where the Areopagus sermon awaited. The persistent persecution, far from halting the gospel, providentially advanced it to new fields.
14:
"And then immediately the brethren sent away Paul to go as it were to the sea: but Silas and Timotheus abode there still." The witnesses note Paul's prudent departure from Berea. The grammatical voices note the brethren's care. As at Thessalonica, the brethren swiftly sent Paul away for his safety, while Silas and Timothy remained to continue the work.

On "as it were to the sea," the witnesses note the route. Some read it as a feint or as the actual route by sea to Athens; the phrase indicates Paul's direction toward the coast.

The witnesses note the providence: Paul sent on to Athens (v.15), while Silas and Timothy stayed to establish the Berean church. The verse displays the prudent departure and the division of labor.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the brethren swiftly sent Paul away for safety (as at Thessalonica), directing him toward the sea (the route to Athens), while Silas and Timothy remained at Berea to continue the work. The verse displays the prudent departure: Paul sent on, the others staying. The verse marks the division of labor: Paul to Athens, Silas and Timothy to establish the Berean church. The verse displays the brethren's care: protecting Paul by a swift departure. The verse displays the providence in the move: Paul sent toward Athens, the great philosophical center, where the Areopagus sermon awaited. The verse displays the continuation of the mission: Silas and Timothy remaining to confirm the Berean believers. The prudent departure, the division of labor, display the wise management of the mission under persecution. The verse displays the providence sending Paul to Athens while preserving the Berean work. The verse marks the transition to the Athens ministry: Paul sent toward the sea, the others remaining. The verse displays both the cost of the gospel (Paul again driven on) and the providence that carried it forward — to Athens, where Paul would confront the philosophers, and where the great sermon on the Areopagus would be preached. The verse sets up Paul's solitary arrival at Athens (vv.15-16) and the encounter with the Epicureans and Stoics.
15:
"And they that conducted Paul brought him unto Athens: and receiving a commandment unto Silas and Timotheus for to come to him with all speed, they departed." The witnesses note Paul's arrival at Athens. The grammatical voices note the escort and the message. The brethren conducted Paul to Athens and carried back his command for Silas and Timothy to join him quickly.

On Athens, the witnesses note its fame. Athens, the great center of Greek philosophy, learning, and art, was the intellectual capital of the ancient world. Paul, arriving alone, would soon confront its philosophers.

The witnesses note Paul's desire for his companions. He sent for Silas and Timothy to join him, both for fellowship and for the work. The verse displays Paul's arrival at the philosophical capital and his summons to his companions.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the brethren conducted Paul to Athens (the great center of philosophy and learning) and carried back his command for Silas and Timothy to join him quickly. The verse displays Paul's arrival at the intellectual capital: alone, soon to confront the philosophers. The verse marks the transition to the Athens ministry: Paul at the philosophical center, summoning his companions. The verse displays the fame of Athens: the capital of Greek thought, art, and religion, where Paul would preach the gospel. The verse displays Paul's desire for fellowship and help: sending for Silas and Timothy. The verse displays the providence bringing Paul to Athens: the apostle of Christ confronting the wisdom of Greece. The arrival at Athens, the summons to his companions, set the scene for the great encounter: Paul amid the philosophers, the gospel confronting the wisdom of the world. The verse displays the significance of the moment: the apostle at the heart of Greek learning, about to preach Christ and the resurrection. The verse marks the beginning of the Athens episode, which would culminate in the Areopagus sermon — Paul's masterful address to the philosophers. The arrival at Athens, alone amid the monuments of pagan wisdom and idolatry, sets the stage for the great confrontation between the gospel and Greek philosophy.
16:
"Now while Paul waited for them at Athens, his spirit was stirred in him, when he saw the city wholly given to idolatry." The witnesses note Paul's holy indignation at the idolatry. On "his spirit was stirred," the grammatical voices note the strong emotion. Robertson and others note the verb (paroxyno) denotes a sharp, deep stirring — Paul provoked, grieved, roused at the sight of the idolatry.

On "wholly given to idolatry," the witnesses note the city full of idols. Athens was famous for its multitude of statues, temples, and altars — it was said to be easier to find a god than a man there. The whole city was given to idol-worship.

Chrysostom and the witnesses note the contrast: the apostle, amid the splendid art and culture, grieved not at the beauty but at the idolatry — the souls in darkness. The verse displays Paul's holy zeal stirred by the idolatry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, waiting at Athens, was deeply stirred (provoked, grieved, Robertson) at the sight of the city wholly given to idolatry — full of statues, temples, and altars, more gods than men. Chrysostom notes the contrast: the apostle grieved not at the splendid art but at the idolatry, the souls in darkness. The verse displays Paul's holy indignation: his spirit roused at the dishonor done to God. The verse marks the apostle's zeal: stirred by the idolatry, moved to preach. The verse displays the right response to idolatry: not admiration of the art but grief at the spiritual darkness. The verse displays the heart of the evangelist: provoked by the dishonor of God and the lostness of souls. The verse displays the contrast between the world's view (admiring the splendid culture) and the apostle's (grieving at the idolatry). The verse displays the source of Paul's preaching: not curiosity or scholarly interest, but holy zeal stirred by the idolatry. The verse displays the model for Christians amid a pagan culture: grieved at the spiritual darkness, moved to bear witness. Paul's stirred spirit, his holy indignation at the idolatry, displays the heart of the true minister — not admiring the world's grandeur but grieving at its lostness, and moved thereby to preach Christ. The verse sets up Paul's reasoning in the synagogue and market (v.17) and the encounter with the philosophers.
17:
"Therefore disputed he in the synagogue with the Jews, and with the devout persons, and in the market daily with them that met with him." The witnesses note Paul's reasoning in synagogue and marketplace. The grammatical voices note the two venues. Paul reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and God-fearers (his regular method), and also in the agora (marketplace) daily with whoever he met.

On the marketplace, the witnesses note the new approach. The agora was the center of Athenian life, where Socrates had reasoned with passers-by. Paul, following this custom, engaged the people daily — adapting his method to the philosophical city.

The witnesses note Paul's diligence and adaptability: reasoning in the synagogue and the marketplace, with Jews, God-fearers, and Gentiles. The verse displays the apostle's tireless engagement at Athens.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul reasoned in the synagogue with the Jews and God-fearers (his regular method) and daily in the marketplace (the agora) with whoever he met — adapting to the philosophical city, where Socrates had reasoned with passers-by. The verse displays Paul's diligent, adaptable engagement: synagogue and marketplace, Jews and Gentiles. The verse marks the apostle's tireless reasoning at Athens: engaging all who would hear. The verse displays the adaptability of the evangelist: following the Athenian custom of marketplace discourse. The verse displays the breadth of Paul's outreach: the synagogue (Jews and God-fearers) and the agora (the philosophers and people). The verse displays the diligence of the apostle: reasoning daily, not waiting for opportunity but seizing it. The verse displays the providence in the marketplace reasoning: it would bring Paul to the attention of the philosophers (v.18) and to the Areopagus. The verse displays the apostle's method at Athens: combining his regular synagogue ministry with the new marketplace engagement suited to the city. Paul's tireless reasoning, in synagogue and agora, displays his zeal and adaptability — meeting the Athenians where they were, engaging the philosophers on their own ground. The verse sets up the encounter with the Epicureans and Stoics (v.18) and the invitation to the Areopagus. The verse displays the apostle's diligent, adaptable witness at the great center of Greek thought.
18:
"Then certain philosophers of the Epicureans, and of the Stoicks, encountered him. And some said, What will this babbler say? other some, He seemeth to be a setter forth of strange gods: because he preached unto them Jesus, and the resurrection." The witnesses note the encounter with the philosophers. On the two schools, the grammatical voices note their character. The Epicureans denied providence and a future life, holding pleasure the chief good (practical atheism, Barnes, Ellicott); the Stoics held a pantheistic providence, fate, and self-sufficient virtue (pride, Calvin). Robertson: the Stoic "the Pharisee of philosophy," the Epicurean "the Sadducee of philosophy."

On "babbler" (spermologos), the witnesses note the contempt. The word means "seed-picker" — a bird picking up scraps, hence a retailer of secondhand scraps of knowledge, a charlatan. The philosophers scorned Paul as a quack.

On "setter forth of strange gods," the witnesses note the misunderstanding. Barnes, Robertson, and others note they took "Jesus and the Anastasis (resurrection)" for two new deities — Jesus and the goddess Resurrection. The same charge (introducing new gods) had condemned Socrates. The verse displays the philosophers' scornful encounter and their misunderstanding.

The witnesses agree: the Epicurean and Stoic philosophers encountered Paul — the Epicureans denying providence and a future life (practical atheists, holding pleasure chief, Barnes), the Stoics holding fate and self-sufficient virtue (proud, Calvin), the Sadducee and Pharisee of philosophy (Robertson). Some scorned Paul as a "babbler" (spermologos, a seed-picking bird, a retailer of scraps, a charlatan); others took him for a setter forth of strange gods, misunderstanding "Jesus and the Anastasis (resurrection)" as two new deities (Barnes, Robertson) — the charge that had condemned Socrates. The verse displays the scornful encounter: the philosophers' contempt and misunderstanding. The verse marks the confrontation of the gospel with Greek philosophy: Paul amid the Epicureans and Stoics. The verse displays the world's wisdom against the gospel: the philosophers scorning the preacher of Christ. The verse displays the misunderstanding: the resurrection taken as a goddess, the gospel mistaken for new idolatry. The encounter, the scorn, the misunderstanding display the gulf between the wisdom of the world and the gospel of Christ. The verse displays the centrality of Jesus and the resurrection in Paul's preaching, even mistaken by the Greeks. The verse sets up the invitation to the Areopagus (v.19) and the great sermon.
19:
"And they took him, and brought him unto Areopagus, saying, May we know what this new doctrine, whereof thou speakest, is?" The witnesses note Paul brought to the Areopagus. On the Areopagus, the grammatical voices note its character. Barnes and Ellicott note it was Mars' Hill, the seat of the ancient and revered Athenian court (the Areopagus), which had jurisdiction over religious matters. Whether Paul was brought for a formal hearing or simply to a place of discourse is debated (Robertson, Ellicott).

On "took him," the witnesses note no necessary violence. Robertson: "no necessary sense of violence" — they brought him, perhaps for examination concerning his new teaching, not as a criminal trial.

On the inquiry, the witnesses note the courteous (if slightly sarcastic) request. They asked to know this "new doctrine" — the Athenian curiosity for novelty (v.21). The verse displays Paul brought before the council to explain his teaching.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the philosophers brought Paul to the Areopagus (Mars' Hill, the seat of the ancient Athenian court with jurisdiction over religion, Barnes, Ellicott), asking to know his "new doctrine" — whether for a formal hearing or a place of discourse is debated (Robertson, Ellicott), but no necessary violence, the request courteous (if slightly sarcastic), reflecting the Athenian curiosity (v.21). The verse displays Paul brought before the council to explain his teaching. The verse marks the setting of the great sermon: the Areopagus, the revered Athenian tribunal. The verse displays the opportunity given Paul: to expound his doctrine before the philosophers and notables of Athens. The verse displays the providence in the invitation: Paul brought to the most famous court in the world to preach the gospel. The verse displays the Athenian inquiry: courteous yet curious, seeking the novelty. The verse displays the platform afforded the apostle: the Areopagus, where his masterful address would be delivered. The verse marks the grand occasion: Paul amid the philosophers and judges, about to preach Christ and the resurrection. The bringing to the Areopagus, the inquiry into the new doctrine, set the scene for one of the greatest sermons in Scripture — Paul's address to the Athenians on the unknown God, the Creator, and the risen Christ. The verse displays the providence preparing the platform for the gospel's proclamation at the heart of Greek wisdom.
20:
"For thou bringest certain strange things to our ears: we would know therefore what these things mean." The brief verse notes the Athenians' curiosity. The grammatical voices note the "strange things" (xenizonta, surprising or shocking things). The doctrine Paul preached was new and startling to Athenian ears, and they wished to understand it.

On "we would know," the witnesses note the polite inquiry. The request was courteous, expressing the desire to understand the unfamiliar teaching. The Athenian appetite for novelty (v.21) drove the inquiry.

The witnesses note the opportunity this gave Paul: invited to explain his "strange" doctrine, he could expound the gospel fully. The verse displays the Athenian curiosity inviting Paul's exposition.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the Athenians, finding Paul's doctrine "strange" (surprising, shocking, xenizonta), wished to understand it — a polite inquiry driven by their appetite for novelty (v.21). The verse displays the Athenian curiosity: inviting Paul to explain his unfamiliar teaching. The verse marks the opportunity afforded Paul: to expound the gospel fully before the council. The verse displays the strangeness of the gospel to Greek ears: the doctrine of Christ and the resurrection startling and new. The verse displays the providence in the curiosity: the Athenian love of novelty opening the door for the gospel. The verse displays the invitation to Paul: to make known the "strange things" — the gospel of the risen Christ. The verse, brief as it is, marks the request that gave Paul his platform: the Athenians wishing to know his doctrine. The verse displays the curiosity that, though shallow (v.21), providentially provided the occasion for the great sermon. The verse displays the gulf between the gospel and Greek thought: the doctrine of Christ "strange" to the philosophers. Yet the curiosity, however shallow, opened the door for Paul to proclaim the unknown God, the Creator, and the risen Lord. The verse sets up the Areopagus sermon (vv.22-31): Paul, invited to explain his "strange" doctrine, expounding the gospel to the Athenians. The verse displays the providence using even idle curiosity to give the apostle a hearing.
21:
"(For all the Athenians and strangers which were there spent their time in nothing else, but either to tell, or to hear some new thing.)" The witnesses note Luke's characterization of the Athenians. The grammatical voices note the famous description. Athens, the intellectual capital, was given to idle curiosity — its people and the foreigners there spent their time hearing and telling novelties.

On the love of novelty, the witnesses note the vice. Calvin and others note this curiosity was a vain, restless appetite — not a genuine desire for truth but a craving for the latest news. The Athenian character was famous for this (attested by Greek and Latin writers).

The witnesses note the bearing on the gospel: the Athenians heard Paul out of curiosity, not earnest seeking. Yet the providence used even this. The verse displays the Athenian appetite for novelty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Luke characterizes the Athenians (and the foreigners there) as spending their time in nothing but telling or hearing some new thing — the famous Athenian love of novelty, a vain, restless curiosity (Calvin), not a genuine desire for truth but a craving for the latest news, attested by ancient writers. The verse displays the Athenian appetite for novelty: idle curiosity, not earnest seeking. The verse marks the disposition that brought Paul a hearing: the love of the new. The verse displays the vice of empty curiosity: hearing and telling novelties, with no settled love of truth. The verse displays the bearing on the gospel: the Athenians heard Paul out of curiosity, not earnest inquiry — explaining the shallow response (vv.32-34). The verse displays the providence using even this: the curiosity giving Paul a platform, though the harvest at Athens would be small. The verse displays the danger of mere curiosity: hearing the gospel as a novelty, not receiving it in earnest. The Athenian appetite for novelty, while opening the door for Paul, also foreshadowed the meager fruit — for those who hear the gospel as mere news seldom believe. The verse displays the contrast with the noble Bereans (v.11): they searched the Scriptures; the Athenians sought novelty. The verse displays the disposition that hears yet does not receive — the idle curiosity that brought Paul a hearing but yielded little fruit. The verse sets up the sermon and its mixed reception.
22:
"Then Paul stood in the midst of Mars' hill, and said, Ye men of Athens, I perceive that in all things ye are too superstitious." The witnesses note the opening of the Areopagus sermon — and the translation debate. The puritan Henry notes this is a sermon to heathens, leading them by the works of providence to the knowledge of the Creator (unlike the synagogue sermons from Scripture).

On "too superstitious," the witnesses debate the tone. Ellicott: the word (deisidaimonesteros) is "not easy to express... 'superstitious' is too strong on the side of blame; 'devout,' on the side of praise"; better, "more fearful of the gods than others" — neutral with a slight touch of disparagement. Robertson notes the conciliatory tone. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke argues strongly for "religious," not "superstitious": Paul, opening his defense, would not insult them, but stated a fact (the Athenians most devoted to religion) and reasoned from it — a captatio benevolentiae to gain a hearing.

The witnesses note Paul's tact: beginning by acknowledging their religiousness, gaining the ears of the philosophers, before leading them to the true God. The verse displays the masterful opening.

The witnesses agree: Paul, standing in the Areopagus, opened his sermon to the heathens (leading them by providence to the Creator, Henry) by observing that the Athenians were "in all things" very religious — and the witnesses debate the tone of deisidaimonesteros: Ellicott and Robertson take it as neutral with a slight touch of disparagement ("more fearful of the gods"), a conciliatory opening; Clarke argues strongly for "religious," a compliment (not insult) stating a fact to gain a hearing (captatio benevolentiae). The verse displays the masterful opening: Paul acknowledging their religiousness to gain their ears. The verse marks the beginning of the great sermon: tactful, conciliatory, leading toward the true God. The verse displays Paul's wisdom: beginning where the Athenians were (their religiousness), not with denunciation. The verse displays the apostle's method with the heathen: from the works of providence and their own devotion to the knowledge of the Creator. The translation debate — "superstitious" vs "religious"/"more fearful of the gods" — turns on the tone: most take it as a tactful, neutral-to-slightly-disparaging opening, neither flattery nor insult. The verse displays the consummate skill of the apostle, opening his address with a point of contact that would gain a hearing before leading the Athenians to the unknown God (v.23).
23:
"For as I passed by, and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with this inscription, TO THE UNKNOWN GOD. Whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you." The witnesses note Paul's masterful use of the altar to the unknown God. On the altar, the grammatical voices note its historical reality. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott cite ancient testimony (Pausanias, Philostratus, Diogenes Laertius — the Epimenides story) of altars to unknown gods at Athens, erected when the people knew not which deity to thank or appease.

On Paul's use of it, the witnesses note the consummate skill. Robertson: "a most clever use of it with consummate skill." By this, Paul eluded the charge of introducing new gods (v.18): he proclaimed not a new god but the one they already, ignorantly, worshipped. Calvin notes Paul did not flatter their worship but took from their own confession of ignorance free matter for teaching.

On "whom ye ignorantly worship, him declare I," the witnesses note the natural theology and its limit. The Athenians confessed their ignorance by the altar; Paul would declare the true God. Their need of instruction opened the way for his preaching. The verse displays the masterful point of contact.

The witnesses agree: Paul, having seen an altar "TO THE UNKNOWN GOD" (a historically attested Athenian practice, from the Epimenides story and others, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott), used it with consummate skill (Robertson) — proclaiming not a new god (eluding the charge of v.18) but the one they ignorantly worshipped, the true God he would declare. Calvin notes Paul did not flatter their worship but took from their confessed ignorance free matter for teaching. The verse displays the masterful point of contact: the altar of ignorance becoming the bridge to the true God. The verse marks the brilliant strategy: turning their own confession of ignorance into the occasion for the gospel. The verse displays Paul's method: meeting the Athenians on their own ground, using their altar as a starting-point. The verse displays the natural theology and its limit: the Athenians had a sense of the divine (the altar) but knew not the true God; Paul would declare him. The verse displays the apostle's skill: not denouncing their idolatry first, but using their acknowledged ignorance to introduce the true God. The altar to the unknown God, masterfully employed, displays Paul's wisdom in finding a point of contact — proclaiming the God they sought but knew not. The verse displays the model of evangelism: meeting people where they are, using what they have, leading them to the true God. The verse sets up the body of the sermon: the declaration of the Creator (vv.24-29).
24:
"God that made the world and all things therein, seeing that he is Lord of heaven and earth, dwelleth not in temples made with hands;" The witnesses note Paul's declaration of God the Creator. On "God that made the world," the grammatical voices note the foundation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul begins with the one God who created all — opposing the Epicurean view (matter eternal, no creation) and the polytheism of the many gods. Robertson notes Paul echoes Isaiah 42:5.

On "dwelleth not in temples made with hands," the witnesses note the echo of Stephen (7:48). Ellicott notes the persecutor Saul had heard this from Stephen; now he proclaims it. The Creator, Lord of heaven and earth, cannot be confined to temples.

The Reformed Calvin notes the method: Paul, dealing with heathens, argues from nature — beginning with the definition of God (the Creator) to prove how he ought to be worshipped. Knowing the true God is the foundation of true worship. The verse displays the declaration of the Creator against idolatry.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul declared God the Creator of the world and Lord of heaven and earth, who dwells not in temples made with hands — opposing the Epicurean view (no creation) and polytheism (Barnes, Robertson, echoing Isaiah 42:5), and echoing Stephen's words (7:48) that Saul had once heard (Ellicott). Calvin notes the method: arguing from nature, beginning with the definition of God (the Creator) to establish true worship. The verse displays the declaration of the Creator: the one God who made all, not confined to temples. The verse marks the foundation of the sermon: the true God, Creator and Lord, against idolatry. The verse displays the apostle's method with the heathen: arguing from creation to the Creator. The verse displays the truth against idolatry: the Maker of all cannot dwell in man-made temples. The verse displays the foundation of true worship: knowing the true God, the Creator, Lord of heaven and earth. The declaration of the Creator, dwelling not in temples, strikes at the heart of Greek idolatry (the temples, the statues). The verse displays the echo of Stephen: the truth that had enraged Saul now proclaimed by Paul. The verse displays the apostle's argument from nature: the works of creation revealing the Creator, who transcends all temples. The verse sets up the further argument: God needs nothing (v.25), made all nations (v.26), and is near to all (v.27).
25:
"Neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing, seeing he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things;" The witnesses note God's self-sufficiency against ritualism. On "neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing," the grammatical voices note the argument against the notion that the gods need men's offerings. Barnes notes the heathen idea that the gods were fed or nourished by sacrifices; Paul refutes it — the Creator needs nothing.

On "he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things," the witnesses note God the Giver. Barnes: "He is the Source of life... He not only originally gave life, but he gives it at each moment." God gives all; he receives nothing he needs. Clarke notes worship is for man's benefit, not God's need.

The Reformed Calvin notes the argument against ceremonialism: men cannot give God anything, for they have nothing but what he gives. The verse displays God the self-sufficient Giver, against the notion that worship supplies his need.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: God is not served by men's hands as though needing anything, for he gives to all life, breath, and all things — refuting the heathen notion that the gods need offerings (Barnes), God the Giver who needs nothing (the source of life at each moment, Barnes); worship is for man's benefit, not God's need (Clarke). Calvin notes men cannot give God anything, having nothing but what he gives. The verse displays God the self-sufficient Giver: needing nothing, giving all. The verse marks the argument against ritualism: God is not supplied by men's worship. The verse displays the true relation of God and man: God the Giver, man the receiver. The verse displays the refutation of the heathen (and all) notion that worship supplies God's need: the Creator needs nothing. The verse displays the dependence of all on God: life, breath, and all things his gift. The verse displays the absurdity of thinking God dependent on men's offerings: he who gives all needs nothing. The verse displays the foundation of true worship: not supplying God's need (he has none) but acknowledging dependence on the Giver. The verse displays God's self-sufficiency and bounty: needing nothing, giving everything. The argument strikes at the heart of pagan sacrifice (feeding the gods) and at all notions that worship benefits God. The verse displays the apostle's teaching: God the all-sufficient Giver, on whom all life depends, who needs nothing from his creatures. The verse sets up the further argument: God made all nations and determines their times and bounds (v.26).
26:
"And hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth, and hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation;" The witnesses note the unity of mankind and the providence over the nations. On "of one blood all nations," the grammatical voices note the unity of the race. Barnes: "all the human family are descended from the same ancestor" — striking at slavery, oppression, and pride of race. Ellicott notes this struck at the Greek distinction between Greek and barbarian; Paul's cosmopolitanism rises above Jew and Greek (Robertson).

On "the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation," the witnesses note the divine providence over history. Robertson: God's hand in the history of all nations, not blind chance or Stoic fate. The Reformed Calvin emphasizes God's counsel, not bare foreknowledge: "he placeth the cause of those things which fall out, in his counsel and beck... God doth govern all events from out of his holy sanctuary." The rise and fall of nations is by God's appointment.

The witnesses note the bearing on idolatry: the one God made all nations; their diverse religions are a corruption (Calvin). The verse displays the unity of mankind and God's sovereign providence over the nations.

The witnesses agree: God made of one blood all nations (the unity of the race, striking at racial pride and oppression, Barnes; rising above the Greek/barbarian distinction, Ellicott, Robertson) and determined their appointed times and the bounds of their habitation — God's sovereign providence over history, not blind chance or Stoic fate (Robertson). Calvin emphasizes God's counsel, not bare foreknowledge: God governs all events by his decree, appointing the times and bounds of every nation. The verse displays the unity of mankind and the divine providence over the nations. The verse marks Paul's philosophy of history: God ruling the rise and fall of nations. The verse displays the oneness of the human race: all descended from one, all equal before God. The verse displays God's sovereign providence: the times and bounds of the nations fixed by his counsel. The verse displays the refutation of Greek pride: not Greek and barbarian, but one race under one God. The verse displays the doctrine of providence (and, for Calvin, predestination): God's counsel, not chance or fate, governing history. The unity of mankind and the sovereign providence over the nations display the one God ruling all peoples. The verse displays the bearing on idolatry: the diverse religions of the nations are a corruption of the one true worship. The verse sets up the purpose of God's providence: that the nations should seek him (v.27).
27:
"That they should seek the Lord, if haply they might feel after him, and find him, though he be not far from every one of us:" The witnesses note the purpose of God's providence — that men should seek him. On "seek the Lord," the grammatical voices note the design. Barnes: God placed men in the world that they should contemplate his works and come to know him (Romans 1:19-20). The end of life is to seek God.

On "feel after him" (groping), the witnesses note the image. Robertson and Ellicott note the word pictures the blind groping of the darkened heathen mind after God — like feeling along a wall in the dark. The Gentiles, without revelation, grope after the God they have lost.

On "not far from every one of us," the witnesses note God's nearness. The Referenced Calvin notes God offers himself to be found, near to all — so that men's blindness is inexcusable. Ellicott notes the appeal to conscience: God near in the depths of each being. Calvin notes that though men grope, they cannot find God by nature alone without the Spirit's illumination, yet are without excuse. The verse displays the search for God and his nearness.

The witnesses agree: the purpose of God's providence is that men should seek him, groping after him (the blind groping of the darkened heathen mind, Robertson, Ellicott) and finding him, for he is not far from any — God placing men in the world to know him (Barnes, Romans 1:19-20). Calvin notes God offers himself near to all, so men's blindness is inexcusable; yet by nature alone, without the Spirit, they cannot truly find him, though they remain without excuse. The verse displays the search for God and his nearness. The verse marks the design of providence: that men should seek and find God. The verse displays the image of groping: the heathen, without revelation, feeling after the God they have lost. The verse displays God's nearness: not far from any, offering himself to be found. The verse displays the inexcusableness of man's blindness: God so near, yet men grope in darkness. The verse displays the limit of natural theology (Calvin): men cannot find God by nature alone without the Spirit, yet are without excuse for not seeking. The search for God, his nearness, and man's groping display both the design of providence (that men should seek God) and the tragedy of the fall (that men grope in darkness). The verse displays the appeal to conscience: God near to all, to be sought and found. The verse sets up the quotation of the poets (v.28): in him we live and move, and we are his offspring.
28:
"For in him we live, and move, and have our being; as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring." The witnesses note God's immanence and Paul's quotation of the Greek poets. On "in him we live, and move, and have our being," the grammatical voices note the dependence on God. Barnes: "the original Fountain of life; and he upholds us each moment." Robertson notes not Stoic pantheism but real immanence — God's sustaining presence. Ellicott notes the three verbs (animal life, emotional nature, essential being) — all from God.

On the quotation, the witnesses note Paul's use of the Greek poets. "We are also his offspring" is from Aratus (a Cilician poet, Paul's countryman) and Cleanthes (the Hymn to Zeus, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson). Paul, acquainted with Greek literature, quotes a pagan poet to make his point — using their own writers against idolatry.

On "his offspring," the witnesses note the sense. Calvin notes the poets spoke of Zeus, but Paul applies the true principle: men are God's offspring by creation, made in his image (not the adoption of believers, but the natural kinship of all men with their Creator). The verse displays God's immanence and the masterful use of the poets.

The witnesses agree: God is so near that in him we live, move, and have our being (real immanence, not Stoic pantheism, Robertson; the three verbs of animal life, emotion, and essence, Ellicott) — and Paul quotes the Greek poets ("we are his offspring," from Aratus, his Cilician countryman, and Cleanthes, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson), using their own writers against idolatry. Calvin notes the poets spoke of Zeus, but Paul applies the true principle: men are God's offspring by creation, made in his image (the natural kinship of all men, not the adoption of believers). The verse displays God's immanence and the masterful use of the poets. The verse marks Paul's learned method: quoting pagan poets to support the truth. The verse displays the dependence of all on God: living, moving, existing in him. The verse displays the apostle's acquaintance with Greek literature: using Aratus and Cleanthes to reach the philosophers. The verse displays the true sense of "offspring": all men by creation are God's children, bearing his image — the ground of the argument against idolatry (v.29). The verse displays the right use of secular learning: Paul drawing from the poets the true principle they had corrupted. The verse displays God's immanence (in him we live) and man's kinship with God (his offspring) — the foundation for the argument that God cannot be like gold or stone (v.29). The verse displays the consummate skill of the apostle, using the Athenians' own poets to lead them toward the true God.
29:
"Forasmuch then as we are the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Godhead is like unto gold, or silver, or stone, graven by art and man's device." The witnesses note the argument against idolatry. On the inference, the grammatical voices note the logic. Barnes: "Since we are formed by God; since we are like him, living and intelligent beings; since we are more excellent than the most precious works of art, it is absurd to suppose that the Source of our existence can be like gold, and silver, and stone." Robertson notes the conclusion drawn from the poets' own words.

On "graven by art and man's device," the witnesses note the folly of idols. The idols of gold, silver, and stone, made by human craft, cannot be the Godhead. If man (God's offspring, living and intelligent) is more excellent than a statue, how much more the Creator.

The Reformed Calvin notes the argument: the soul, bearing God's image, cannot be painted; how much more absurd to paint God. The verse displays the refutation of idolatry from the kinship of man with God.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: since we are God's offspring (made in his image, living and intelligent), we ought not to think the Godhead like gold, silver, or stone, made by human craft — the argument drawn from the poets' own words (Robertson): if man, God's offspring, is more excellent than a statue, how much more the Creator (Barnes). Calvin notes the soul, bearing God's image, cannot be painted; how much more absurd to paint God. The verse displays the refutation of idolatry: from the kinship of man with God. The verse marks the conclusion of the argument: idols cannot be the Godhead, for man himself is more excellent. The verse displays the folly of idolatry: making the Creator like dead, crafted matter. The verse displays the logic: if we are God's offspring, living and intelligent, the Source of our being cannot be like a lifeless image. The verse displays the absurdity of representing God by gold, silver, or stone: the living God cannot be a crafted idol. The verse displays the argument from the dignity of man: bearing God's image, more excellent than any statue, proving the Creator far above all idols. The refutation of idolatry, drawn from the kinship of man with God and from the Athenians' own poets, displays Paul's masterful reasoning. The verse displays the truth against idolatry: the living God cannot be represented by dead matter. The verse strikes at the heart of Greek idolatry (the splendid statues of Phidias). The verse sets up the call to repentance (v.30): the times of ignorance now to be left behind.
30:
"And the times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent:" The witnesses note God's forbearance and the universal call to repent. On "the times of this ignorance God winked at," the grammatical voices debate the sense. Robertson: "overlooked" — not "winked at" (condoning), but a negative force; God objected to the idolatry but did not fully punish it before the gospel. Barnes: "overlooked; connived at; did not come forth to punish... their ignorance was a mitigating circumstance," not innocence. Ellicott notes it as a mitigation of guilt, not concurrence.

On "but now commandeth all men every where to repent," the witnesses note the universal command. The times of ignorance past, God now commands repentance of all — Jew and Gentile, everywhere. Barnes draws the lessons: repentance is a duty, possible, binding on all, necessary to salvation.

The Reformed Calvin notes the forbearance and the urgency: God overlooked the past, but now, when he speaks, men must obey without delay; the ignorance of former times excuses no longer. The verse displays God's forbearance and the universal call to repentance.

The witnesses agree: God overlooked the times of ignorance (not condoning but not fully punishing the idolatry before the gospel, Robertson, Barnes — a mitigation of guilt, not innocence, Ellicott), but now commands all men everywhere to repent. The times of ignorance past, the universal command goes forth: repentance of all, Jew and Gentile (Barnes: a duty, possible, binding, necessary). Calvin notes the forbearance and urgency: God overlooked the past, but now, when he speaks, men must obey without delay. The verse displays God's forbearance and the universal call to repentance. The verse marks the turning-point: from the times of ignorance to the command to repent. The verse displays God's patience: overlooking the past idolatry, not punishing it fully before the light came. The verse displays the universal command: all men everywhere to repent, now that the gospel has come. The verse displays the mitigation of past guilt (ignorance) and the present accountability (the command to repent). The verse displays the urgency: the times of ignorance past, repentance now commanded. God's forbearance toward the past and his present call to repentance display both his patience and his justice. The verse displays the universal scope: all men, everywhere, commanded to repent — the gospel's call to the whole world. The verse sets up the ground of the command: the appointed day of judgment by the risen Christ (v.31).
31:
"Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom he hath ordained; whereof he hath given assurance unto all men, in that he hath raised him from the dead." The witnesses note the judgment and the resurrection as its proof. On "appointed a day," the grammatical voices note the certainty of judgment. Barnes: this is the reason for the command to repent — all must be judged. The day is fixed, certain to come.

On "that man whom he hath ordained," the witnesses note Christ the appointed Judge. Robertson notes Paul adds to the Psalm (9:8) the function of Jesus as Judge (Matthew 25). God has ordained the man (Jesus) to judge the world in righteousness.

On "given assurance... in that he hath raised him from the dead," the witnesses note the resurrection as the proof. Barnes, Clarke, Robertson, and Calvin note the resurrection is the assurance (pistis, a ground of conviction, a proof) that Jesus is the Judge — God confirming it by raising him. Calvin: Christ by his resurrection is declared the Judge of the world. Robertson: Paul comes to the heart of his message, the resurrection the proof of all his claims. The verse displays the judgment, the ordained Judge, and the resurrection as the assurance.

The witnesses agree: God has appointed a day to judge the world in righteousness by the man he ordained (Jesus, the appointed Judge, Robertson, Matthew 25), giving assurance to all by raising him from the dead — the reason for the command to repent (all must be judged, Barnes), the resurrection the proof (pistis, a ground of conviction, Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) that Jesus is the Judge. Calvin: Christ by his resurrection is declared the Judge of the world. Robertson notes Paul here reaches the heart of his message, the resurrection the proof of all his claims (clarifying the misunderstanding of v.18). The verse displays the judgment, the ordained Judge, and the resurrection as the assurance. The verse marks the climax of the sermon: the appointed day, the risen Christ as Judge. The verse displays the ground of the call to repent: the certain judgment by the risen Lord. The verse displays the resurrection as the proof: God confirming Jesus as Judge by raising him. The verse displays the heart of Paul's gospel: the risen Christ, ordained Judge, the resurrection the assurance to all. The judgment, the ordained Judge, and the resurrection-proof display the gospel's solemn warning and its sure foundation. The verse displays the apostle's bold proclamation: the risen Jesus, the Judge of all, the resurrection the guarantee. The verse, the climax of the Areopagus sermon, displays the resurrection as the cardinal truth — the proof of the judgment and the heart of the gospel. The verse sets up the response (v.32): the Greek mockery of the resurrection.
32:
"And when they heard of the resurrection of the dead, some mocked: and others said, We will hear thee again of this matter." The witnesses note the divided response to the resurrection. On "some mocked," the grammatical voices note the Greek scorn. The resurrection of the dead was incredible and absurd to the Greeks (the Epicureans, the Sadducees, the philosophers generally). They mocked at the very mention of it.

Chrysostom and the witnesses note the point of offense: as long as Paul spoke of God and providence, they listened; but the resurrection — the bodily rising — they scorned. The Greeks held the body a prison, not to be raised.

On "we will hear thee again," the witnesses note the deferral. Others, not mocking but not convinced, put off the matter — the polite postponement that often masks unbelief. The verse displays the divided, largely unbelieving response.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: when Paul preached the resurrection, some mocked (the Greek scorn of the bodily resurrection, incredible to the philosophers, Epicureans, and the Greek mind generally) and others deferred ("we will hear thee again") — a polite postponement often masking unbelief. Chrysostom notes the point of offense: they listened to talk of God, but the resurrection they scorned. The verse displays the divided, largely unbelieving response: mockery and deferral. The verse marks the reception of the sermon: the resurrection the stumbling-block. The verse displays the Greek difficulty: the bodily resurrection incredible to those who held the body a prison. The verse displays the two responses of unbelief: open mockery and polite deferral. The verse displays the stumbling-block of the resurrection: the cardinal truth scorned by the wise of the world. The verse displays the cost of preaching the resurrection: the mockery of the philosophers. The verse displays the typical responses to the gospel: derision and procrastination, both forms of unbelief. The divided response — mockery and deferral — displays the world's resistance to the resurrection, the heart of the gospel. The verse displays the meager fruit at Athens: the philosophers mostly unmoved, the resurrection the dividing-line. Yet some would believe (v.34). The verse displays the offense of the resurrection to Greek wisdom and the largely unbelieving response of the Athenian philosophers. The verse sets up Paul's departure (v.33) and the few converts (v.34).
33:
"So Paul departed from among them." The brief verse notes Paul's departure. The grammatical voices note the quiet exit. Robertson: "He walked out never to return to Athens. Had he failed?" Paul left the Areopagus, the sermon ended, the response mostly mockery or deferral.

On the departure, the witnesses note Paul's wisdom. Barnes: "Seeing there was little hope of saving them, it was not his custom to labour long in a barren field." Paul did not linger where the soil was unfruitful.

The witnesses note no condemnation: Paul left freely, the matter not a trial but a hearing. The verse displays the quiet departure after the mixed reception.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: Paul departed from the Areopagus — a quiet exit, the sermon ended, the response mostly mockery or deferral (Robertson: never to return to Athens). Barnes notes Paul's wisdom: not lingering in a barren field where there was little hope. There was no condemnation; Paul left freely, the matter a hearing not a trial. The verse displays the quiet departure after the mixed reception. The verse marks the close of the Athens episode: Paul leaving the council. The verse displays the apostle's prudence: not laboring long where the soil was unfruitful. The verse displays the contrast with the fruitful cities: Athens yielding little, Paul moving on. The verse displays the freedom of the departure: no condemnation, Paul leaving as he came. The verse, brief as it is, marks the end of the great sermon and its largely unfruitful reception. The verse displays the apostle's wisdom: discerning when to stay and when to leave. The quiet departure, after the masterful sermon and the meager response, displays the mystery of the gospel's reception: even the greatest preaching, before the wisest audience, may yield little fruit. The verse displays the providence: Athens not the appointed harvest, Paul moving on to Corinth (ch. 18), where a great church awaited. The verse displays the sober reality: the gospel preached faithfully, yet largely rejected by the philosophers. Paul's departure, free and quiet, marks the close of the Athens ministry and the transition to Corinth.
34:
"Howbeit certain men clave unto him, and believed: among the which was Dionysius the Areopagite, and a woman named Damaris, and others with them." The witnesses note the few converts at Athens. On "certain men clave unto him," the grammatical voices note the genuine fruit. Despite the mockery, some believed — drawn to Paul, embracing the gospel. Ellicott notes the word implies both companionship and conversion.

On Dionysius the Areopagite, the witnesses note the notable convert. As a member of the Areopagus court (which required high rank and age), Dionysius was a man of standing. Barnes notes the conversion of one such man was worth Paul's labor; later tradition made him bishop of Athens.

On Damaris and the others, the witnesses note the harvest, small but real. Ellicott notes the contrast with the "many" at Thessalonica and Berea — Athens yielding few, the city with which Paul had least sympathy. Yet souls were saved. The verse displays the small but genuine fruit at Athens.

The witnesses agree: despite the mockery, certain men clave to Paul and believed — including Dionysius the Areopagite (a member of the revered court, a man of standing, worth Paul's labor, Barnes; later tradition's bishop of Athens), a woman named Damaris, and others. Ellicott notes the contrast with the "many" at Thessalonica and Berea: Athens yielded few, the city with which Paul had least sympathy, yet souls were saved. The verse displays the small but genuine fruit at Athens: a few converts, including a notable judge. The verse marks the harvest, meager but real: some believing amid the mockery. The verse displays the worth of even one soul: the conversion of Dionysius worth the labor (Barnes). The verse displays the contrast with the fruitful cities: Athens the least responsive, yet not wholly barren. The verse displays the genuine fruit: Dionysius, Damaris, and others embracing the gospel. The small harvest at Athens displays both the resistance of the philosophical city and the power of the gospel to save even there. The verse displays the encouragement: even where most reject, some believe; the labor is not in vain. The verse displays the providence: a few souls saved at Athens, the seed of a church. The verse marks the close of the Athens ministry: the sermon preached, the mockery endured, yet some believing. The verse displays the truth that the gospel, even where largely rejected, gathers its own — Dionysius, Damaris, and others, the genuine fruit of Paul's masterful witness at the heart of Greek wisdom. The verse closes the chapter with the small but real harvest, the gospel triumphing in a few even where the many mocked.
